Hi Kelly - the model we released is very slow. We have since sped it up, but it's not yet particularly user friendly.

You can speed up the training(6-7x speedup) of the Allennlp model by replacing the LSTM in the config with this one: 
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/modules/alternating_highway_lstm.py#L116
which uses a custom kernel to implement the interleaved LSTMs.

The config section you need for this looks like:
```
"stacked_encoder": {
    "type": "alternating_highway_lstm_cuda",
    "input_size": 200,
    "hidden_size": 300,
    "num_layers": 8,
    "recurrent_dropout_probability": 0.1
}
```
However, if you use this, please bear in mind:
- You cannot load this model onto a machine which does not have a GPU yet, so it is not very portable.
- It isn't yet included in the pip package.

Ok, whoever's interested, this is ready for another look.  These are the changes since people last looked at it:

- Using a relatively new feature of docker, I was able to combine the two original dockerfiles into a single dockerfile, with an argument for what base to start from.  The default is to build for GPUs, and you can pass `--build-arg from=ubuntu:16.04` to get what used to be `Dockerfile.cpu`.  You must have docker >= 1.17 for this to work, I believe (at least it doesn't work on 1.13, and it looked like the next step up was 1.17).
- I removed the internal conda environment, because it's totally unnecessary - we just use the base environment as we're already inside a container.  This makes all of the `RUN` statements much simpler and easier to parse.
- I switched the `run_with_beaker.sh` script to pass the command to the dockerfile, instead of requiring the dockerfile to already have the command.  The script still needs some work to be more general, but it's at least a starting place if you want to run an experiment.
- I added an option to the training code to not use tqdm.

I successfully started a [beaker job](http://beaker.allenai.org/ex/ex_jn5mj85d2bg7) using this script, and it worked.  Because we're bypassing tqdm, the logs are actually useful for seeing what's going on.

> @jgmize any interest in supporting multiple ELBs with multiple SSL certs. If so I could stop patching my version of Deis to add 2 additional ELBs ;)

The patch in its current form requires that you run an ELB for each additional SSL cert, because once you disable the PROXY protocol globally, you lose the ability to terminate SSL at the deis routers for any domain.

You could create the additional ELBs in cloudformation during the initial creation of the deis cluster, but you cannot add new ones with a cloudformation update without it deleting the entire autoscaling group and all EC2 instances in it-- a lesson I learned the hard way (see #4648).

Instead, we only use CF to create the the first ELB, and create the others outside of CF and attach them to the Deis ASG using the aws cli:

```sh
for ELB in $ELBS; do
    aws elb apply-security-groups-to-load-balancer \
        --load-balancer-name $ELB \
        --security-groups $DEISWEB_SG
    aws autoscaling attach-load-balancers \
        --auto-scaling-group-name $ASG \
        --load-balancer-names $ELB
done
```

So here's what I explained in the meeting yesterday about why this is necessary:

In previous versions, you had to specify an etcd key to pull in both slugrunner and slugbuilder into the builder. However as we all have known by now, `docker pull` requires network bandwidth, which in some cases - like when you're running a local vagrant cluster or DockerHub is down - can be slow at times. Therefore, building the images themselves directly inside builder actually sped up the boot process rather than slowed it down. It also made hacking on the builder a lot easier; one would just modify the source, re-compile builder and their changes were available.

In short, I think it's a *great* thing that it's being built inside the builder. We're looking to improve the builder boot pipeline, however. #3453 is just one step in the right direction, IMO (pushing slugrunner to the registry is probably the slowest operation the builder's doing right now).

It took me until this moment to realize that cChardet is actually wrapping [uchardet-enhanced](https://bitbucket.org/medoc/uchardet-enhanced/overview), which is a much-improved version of the original Mozilla code chardet is currently based on.

The ratio used by cChardet is correct; the problem is with the rest of chardet.  Our other probers are reporting confidences that are too high.  We should switch to treating uchardet-enhanced as the upstream version and pull in all of the changes present there.  That would make it even simpler to merge with cChardet as we've discussed before in #19.

BTW, I'm working on the new (mixed = more than one) language model for latin1 (cp1252) prober, which replaces old one (which is based on very "strange heuristic" for me).  The new model will be based on the statistic probability like other "normal" single byte probers.

@mratsim thanks for chiming in here. I had written up a big comment right before the internet had an outage (traveling) but I think it's well aligned to what you wrote. Here it goes:

As promised to @glaksmono in chat, here's me elaborating on the issue. Let's look at how the constants are used to day and the things that may be wrong with that.

Let's consider `GAS_BALANCE` as an example. This constant was introduced to be used as a gas price for the opcode that reads the balance from an account. It was set to `20`. We can see it being used here to set the gas price of that opcode for the frontier code.

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/931e91531389c1a5f26f8f0690d04a7bca65e24a/eth/constants.py#L44

However, retrieving the balance doesn't actually cost `20` gas anymore. It costs `400` as specified in the tangerine whistle fork. However, there was a *new* constant `GAS_BALANCE_EIP150` being introduced for that which lives in the tangerine whistle specific constants.

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/931e91531389c1a5f26f8f0690d04a7bca65e24a/eth/vm/forks/tangerine_whistle/constants.py#L13

My initial reaction was: Let's clean up `eth.constants` and move all things that are only used within a specific fork into the constants of that specific fork. But I think there's a problem with that approach as well.

There are more generic constants such as `GAS_VERYLOW` which are used by many different forks. But then `GAS_LOW` which falls into the same category is only used in the frontier fork. That's more or less random which means, just looking at the usage side doesn't give us a strong indicator of how to organize these constants.

And then there's another category. A constant such as `GAS_SSET` isn't used by any fork but inside `eth.vm.logic` instead.

With that in mind, let's try something else.

Let's create a new class `BaseConstants`  in `eth.vm.forks.core` and define it as such:

```python

class BaseConstants:

    GAS_LOW = 5
    ...
    GAS_BALANCE = 20
    ...

```

And then instead of the forks maintaining plain old constants in a separate file, let them have their inherited versions of the `BaseConstants`.

```python

class TangerineWhistleConstants(BaseConstants):

    GAS_BALANCE = 400

```

Instead of introducing a new, different constant to adjust the gas price of the balance opcode, the fork would just redefine `GAS_BALANCE` with a different value.

With that in mind, let's derive some rules on how to use organize constants under that model.

1. **Every** constant that has been used in the very first version (Frontier) already, goes into `BaseConstants`

2. Constants that just redefine existing constants get dropped (e.g. `GAS_BALANCE_EIP150`) and instead, in the inherited `ForknameConstants`, the value of the original constant is overwritten.

3. New constants *may be* introduced in `ForknameConstants` if they describe things that would not make sense in `BaseConstants` as they aren't generic and rather describe fork specific things.

4. The `ForknameConstants` family of classes *must never* be imported outside of `eth.vm.forks`. E.g. `eth.vm.logic` can only import `BaseConstants` but not `TangerineWhistleConstants`

5. If a function inside `eth.vm.logic` *wants* to import from a fork specific constant, then that is a clear signal that the function either needs to be moved into a fork specific place (which is what @pipermerriam  was suggesting) or that it needs to be refactored to accept the right constants to get injected.

Alright, I *think* this may be good enough to merge:

- I moved the helper function to `trinity.utils.async_iter`
- I got rid of that hardcoded `print` by allowing to pass a `debug_fn` in the `__init__` of `AsyncProcessRunner`
- I turned `iterate_stderr` and `iterate_stdout` into properties `stderr` and `stdout`
- I reduced the concurrency of **only these tests** to one process to not run into errors due to multiple Trinity instances competing for the same ports

Regarding, gracefully killing the Trinity process, let me echo what I wrote in a comment before.

I *think* there is nothing we can gain from sending a `SIGTERM` followed by a `SIGKILL` after some timeout. While this is of course the right way of shutting Trinity down for a normal user, I believe that for these *throwaway* instances, there is nothing to gain from that. At worst, it will just make these test run slower as we have to wait longer on each shutdown.

Also since we are using `asyncio.subprocess.Process` we can't use `join` to wait on the shutdown and I believe waiting in an async fashion would complicate the tear down of the tests.

So, in general, I think this would be **good enough** to merge. Obviously, there's a lot left to do and in fact I've already started on more complex tests that spin up multiple Trinity instances in parallel to test `web3` interaction via `trinity attach`.

Issues like this crop up in the code often: should there or should there not be a trailing slash? 

The problem with the original code is that it made no assertion either way, which means that cache_fn_url would actually produce a different result whether or not the slash was present.

So I am not necessarily in support of restoring the old behavior. However, if we can establish that the trailing slash _was_ present most of the time, then I would support doing this instead:
```
url = url.rstrip('/') + '/'
```

I'm -1 against removing them completely based on the arguments above.  If we can believe we completely understand the "few cases" where file locks are doing a useful job, then we should either narrow down the locking behavior to just those cases, or otherwise guard against them or warn the user.

We can be overly cautious; we don't have to be perfect.  But what we *can't* do is create situations where things "mysteriously" work or don't work.  That erodes user confidence in the tool in a deep and subtle way that is very, very hard to earn back.

Would you rather have a slow car that just works *all of the time*, or a super fast car that randomly breaks down on the highway, when the sun strikes it at a particular angle, when a certain song comes on the radio, etc.?  For a tool that people rely on, predictability is the single biggest feature.

One thing that might cause this sort of error is if files are in use by
some running Python process.  Please make sure to kill all running IPython
processes before trying to update.  To be completely sure, I guess you
could reboot your computer, but that should not really be necessary.  Also
avoid ever doing any "sudo" commands (pip or conda) - this will leave files
with root permissions, leading to these sorts of errors.

@kayhan-batmanghelich When trying to use matplotlib in a virtual environment I changed my bashrc file as suggested [here](http://matplotlib.org/faq/virtualenv_faq.html).  To use the anaconda version of ipython you could change the path to where ipython is installed. Which is usually ~/anaconda/bin/ipython
So instead of: 

`function frameworkpython {
    if [[ ! -z "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]]; then
        PYTHONHOME=$VIRTUAL_ENV /usr/local/bin/python "$@"
    else
        /usr/local/bin/python "$@"
    fi
}`

do the following:

`function frameworkpython {
    if [[ ! -z "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]]; then
        PYTHONHOME=$VIRTUAL_ENV ~/anaconda/bin/ipython "$@"
    else
        ~/anaconda/bin/ipython "$@"
    fi
}`

As stated by the FAQ this is a better way to use a "framework" python in virtualenvs, this way you don't have to change it for every virtualenv seperately.

I see what is happening. By default, the patchelf logic in conda-build only looks in `lib/`, but your libgfortran is in `lib64`. There are two ways to fix this:

- Don't use `lib65` (i.e., symlink `lib64` to `lib` or use relevant build flags to always use `lib` instead of `lib64`).
- Use the `rpaths` flag in the meta.yaml, like

```yaml
build:
  rpaths:
    - lib/
    - lib64/
```

This will tell conda build to add `lib64` to the libraries using patchelf.

I would suggest the first option if possible, since it's simpler.

@kraigher , I've checked bsub and, although similar, it is overkill. Because it is meant for HPC with multiple physical devices, an additional orchestration service (LSF) must be run. My proposal is more straighforward because all the containers are expected to be executed in the same machine, so no additional service is required. Well, docker is the service, somehow, as `docker exec` works for both local containers and services in a swarm cluster. But I'll focus on the local case, which is easier to follow. Four functionally equivalent examples are shown [below](#examples): 1. All the commands at the same time with local tools. 2. All the commands at the same time in a temporal container (same as #323, but without VUnit). 3. All the commands at the same time in a named container running in the background. 4. Commands one-by-one in a named container running in the background. I want to achieve 2, 3 or 4 with VUnit. I will focus on 4, as I think it is the less invasive approach from VUnit's perspective: - Create the container and keep it running in the background, before calling VUnit or as a pre-task hook. - Intercept any `ghdl ` call from VUnit and decorate as `docker exec -t -w bash -c 'ghdl '`. - Stop and remove the container when VUnit exits or as a post-task hook. That's quite easy to achieve, as long as all the sources and products are kept in the single folder that is shared between VUnit and the container with GHDL. However, VUnit requires additional external sources to be built. E.g., for example/vhdl/array_axis_vcs more than 100 files from `usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/vunit_hdl-3.0.3rc0-py3.6.egg/vunit/vhdl/` are compiled. There are two solutions: - Share two folders between VUnit and the GHDL container: the project folder and the installation path of VUnit sources. - Share only the project folder and make VUnit copy the required sources there. Unless you tell me not to do so, I'd go with the first option, because copying the same sources to every project makes little sense to me. As a result, the command 'decoration' is required to not only prepend and append some args, but also to rewrite the two paths that might appear in ``. # Interception/decoration script Shall all be done without any modification to VUnit, this can be a local prototype of the interception script: ``` bash $ mv /usr/local/bin/ghdl /usr/local/bin/ghdlbin $ vim /usr/local/bin/ghdl #!/bin/sh /usr/local/bin/ghdlback $@ :wq ``` In order to see the exact commands that are executed: ``` bash #!/bin/sh if [ "$1" != "--version" ]; then echo "$@" fi /usr/local/bin/ghdlback $@ ``` and we get: ``` can either hardcode or use regexps to replace those with `MYWORKDIR` and `MYVUDIR`, that correspond to the paths inside the container. But it would be cleaner if we could instruct VUnit to use `MYWORKDIR` and `MYVUDIR` only for external calls. Note that it should keep using `WORKDIR` and `VUDIR` internally. If this is already supported, please let me know. # Examples: from local to `docker run` to `docker exec` ``` bash git clone https://github.com/1138-4EB/hwd-ide cd hwd-ide/examples/full_adder #--- #--- All the commands at the same time with local tools #--- mkdir -p output cd output ghdl -a ../hdl/*.vhd ghdl -r adder_tb --vcd=adder.vcd cd .. ls -la output rm -rf output #--- #--- All the commands at the same time in a temporal container #--- docker run --rm -t \ -v /$(pwd)://src \ -w //src \ ghdl/ghdl:stretch-mcode \ bash -c '\ mkdir -p output && \ cd output && \ ghdl -a ../hdl/*.vhd && \ ghdl -r adder_tb --vcd=adder.vcd\ ' ls -la output rm -rf output #--- #--- All the commands at the same time in a named container running in the background #--- # First, create the container and keep it running in the background docker run --name ghdl-sim -d \ -v /$(pwd)://src \ ghdl/ghdl:stretch-mcode \ tail -f /dev/null # Then, exec commands inside docker exec -t \ -w //src \ ghdl-sim \ bash -c '\ mkdir -p output && \ cd output && \ ghdl -a ../hdl/*.vhd && \ ghdl -r adder_tb --vcd=adder.vcd\ ' # Last, stop and remove the container docker rm -f ghdl-sim ls -la output rm -rf output #--- #--- Commands one-by-one in a named container running in the background #--- # First, create the container and keep it running in the background docker run --name ghdl-sim -d \ -v /$(pwd)://src \ ghdl/ghdl:stretch-mcode \ tail -f /dev/null # Then, run each command in its own exec docker exec -t -w //src ghdl-sim bash -c '\ mkdir -p output \ ' docker exec -t -w //src/output ghdl-sim bash -c '\ ghdl -a ../hdl/*.vhd \ ' docker exec -t -w //src/output ghdl-sim bash -c '\ ghdl -r adder_tb --vcd=adder.vcd \ ' # Last, stop and remove the container docker rm -f ghdl-sim ls -la output rm -rf output ```

```yaml
  - id: yapf
    entry: bash -c 'yapf "$@"; git add -u' --
```

(note: using bash will potentially reduce portability)

Determined a cause for connection issue with "WebSockets" Android app. **In short: it was not an aiohttp issue.**

The WebSockets implementation used in "WebSockets" Android app (and probably only in this app) doesn't conform RFC 7230. This application was searching for a `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` header, case sensitively and thus was unable to find a correct `Sec-Websocket-Accept` (note the small `s` letter), sent by aiohttp.

Considering that there is a client-side issue and that there is a client which implemented the protocol improperly, the original issue is not an issue of aiohttp. But if anybody is curious about the fix, here is a small hack for this: https://github.com/s-kostyuk/aiohttp/commit/3fe2336aca0004f3564ca7c3731fea04926f8177 (available also as an [image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5122578/39672055-c51a9ae2-512b-11e8-848c-6c7f1503ce32.png) for history purposes).

This issue will be closed to the end of this day if no new information will be found. And, most likely, if something else will be eventually found, then a new issue will be created.

Right now we have no permissions, let's don't discuss it here.
Regarding to url prefix -- it's doable but have own drawback.
Let's consider the following:
```
    routes = web.RoutesDef()

    @routes.get('/path')
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def handler(request):
        pass

    app.router.add_routes(routes, prefix='/base')
```
Final url will be `'/base/path'`. The behavior is pretty simple and obvious.
But my guts feel this is not very good idea.
With `RoutesDef` the route table is scratched over many places in many files.
The most practical way to find proper web handler is grepping the source code by URL.
Prefixes make the process much harder: route definitions may have the same path but different prefixes, it should very common for REST based solutions.
Thus better to have longer paths in decorators for easy searching. Otherwise user should look on two places: decorator and `RoutesDef` registration.

P.S.
I prefer `RoutesDef` to `Routes`: we already have `Route` concept and it's used for **registered** route. But `RoutesDef` is for **unregistered** route definition, let's don't mix them.

Now I think `RouteInfo` namedtuple should be renamed to `RouteDef` (the name is almost newer be used in application code).
More important `router.add_routes()` should renamed `.add_routes_def()`. It's 4 chars longer but supports more native semantics.

The way we have handled this in JupyterLab follow the standard Operation
System model:

* We track things by file extension
* There is a registry of handlers/apps/activities that register as handlers
for a set of file extensions.
* There is a default handlers for each file extension.
* The default handler is used when the user selects (click or double click)
on the file in the file browser.
* Alternate handlers are available in a context menu.

I don't think it is worth implementing all of that in classic notebook, but
the model might help show the way to proceed in notebook.

Brian

Contrary to the previous comment, this is not the same as the "unset field" issue, although it's related. This here is about the serialized representation of empty model instances.

An empty instance will currently be exported as `None`. OP expected to see `{}`.

An instance is empty if
* its class doesn't define any fields (OPs example) or
* none of its fields contains a nonempty value AND `serialize_when_none=False`.

```python
class Test(Model):

    class Options:
        serialize_when_none = False

    l = ListType(IntType())
    s = StringType()
```
```python
>>> t = Test()
>>> t.l = []
>>> print(t.to_primitive())
None
```

The behavior is consistent with `ModelType` fields:


```python
class Outer(Model):
    m = ModelType(Test)
```
```python
>>> o = Outer()
>>> o.m = Test()
>>> print(o.to_primitive())
{'m': None}
```

The question is: is `None` the ideal representation for empty models?

Well, I think `None` makes perfect sense for now, and I don't know how `{}` would be an improvement.

The only argument for `{}` that I can think of is that the current behavior is not entirely consistent between `ModelType` and, e.g., `ListType`:
* `ListType` accepts both `None` and `[]`, and they're serialized as such.
* `ModelType` accepts both `None` and `Foo()`, but they're both serialized as `None`.

My take on this is that the `ModelType` behavior is actually desirable, and it's `ListType` that's acting strange. The fact that `ListType` can already accommodate two different kinds of "empty" means that, once the Undefined feature lands, there will be three kinds.

Therefore, the present issue seems to need addressing only if

* there's currently a scenario where a model or a `ModelType` field will get serialized as `{}`, as that would be inconsistent, or

* there would be some kind of semantic difference between `None` and `{}`, but I don't readily see what that might be.

Hey @fgregg, that would be an honor! Definitely a fan of @datamade's work around e.g. policing, housing, and racial justice (a set of topics very close to my heart).

No objections to the above from me. As to the specific points:

1. A pip-only installation would be amazing. I'd liken the way it currently works to, say, lxml requiring libxml2 to be installed separately. Since libpostal has many bindings, we don't bundle it with any of them, though in general it should be rare that people are using libpostal with multiple language bindings (I can see maybe Python + the Postgres extension). The main concern we have that most other libraries do not is the size of the model downloads. The global parser model currently takes up ~1.8GB of disk/memory, so it's often desirable to specify the location rather than have a potentially non-sensible default (the default datadir in Autotools is something like /usr/local/share, but in a setting like AWS, where root volumes are relatively small, it's often better to use a mounted EBS data volume). It should be fine for the pip install to trigger an install of libpostal, but would still be preferable to have the Python binding play nice with a system installation if the user wants that, especially considering the hefty downloads, and being mindful that not all of our users have access to cheap broadband connections or unrestricted downloads.

    It would definitely be possible to create Debian/Red Hat/Homebrew packages. We don't currently support Windows, but I've removed the problematic dependencies, and one user reported getting it working here: https://github.com/BenK10/libpostal_windows. Would ideally like to get compilation to work with Visual Studio and put up an Appveyor build if Windows support is to be a thing. I personally don't have any Windows machines available at the moment. In any case, if you guys are willing to help with packaging that would be awesome! I've been working mostly on core thus far and for installation have just assumed that a standard Autotools source build (configure/make/make install) is familiar enough to most *nix users.
2. An extended tagset should work without too many changes. The format we use can handle arbitrary tags, especially in terms of the street-level/sub-building components, (though it's best to use our tags or a subset of them for admin components like city/state, etc. which should be compatible with what usaddress uses currently). It might even be possible to derive some OSM-scale training data for the US specifically using the [tiger:name* tags in OSM](https://www.openstreetmap.org/way/38540649), but that's obviously only available in the US. The tagset for libpostal is sort of the lowest common denominator that works internationally. We do have relatively-comprehensive gazetteers of the street types/directionals, etc. for various languages, but many of said languages have their own variations of the "E St" problem (does it mean "East" or just the letter "E"?). It may also be possible to use some of the sources from [OpenAddresses](https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses), which often do split out the directional/street type fields as well, and this is true in some countries outside the US as well. They also have more open licensing than OSM, usually CC-BY or similar.
3. The training data itself would be relatively easy to port. However, there might be a few assumptions that our model makes which would not be as suitable to training on smaller data sets. My goal wasn't really to make a learning framework for address parsing. In fact I usually refer people to usaddress if they want to train their own :). Libpostal was originally more about query analysis i.e. "stick this in front of your geocoder and internationalize all of the things." Nothing about that's incompatible with your use case per se, but our model is definitely built with 10M+ training examples in mind, and there might have to be some tweakable knobs added to feature extraction and the precomputed indices to get it working well on e.g. 10k examples from a user's CSV. A few hyperparameters have been added recently for smaller experiments including number of iterations and a parameter which controls model sparsity. Smaller training sets are also more feasible with the Conditional Random Field as it tends to converge to something reasonable much faster than the greedy averaged perceptron used previously.

The apt, snap, leadership & coordinator layers all rely on the workaround, as well as charms like PostgreSQL which need to initialize the environment before the main loop is invoked, and I recall others copying the workaround. > This does not work when tests from this file are run concurrently. Using a scope="module" fixture does not help either, because module-level fixtures unfortunately do not affect scheduling (I think this would be #18 ), but are re-instantiated on each worker.

Actually using a `scope='module'` fixture together with `loadscope` mode should schedule all tests that use that fixture to be scheduled to the same worker. You are not seeing that happening? All you needs is a single dummy auto-use fixture:

```python
# test_redis.py
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True, scope='module')
def force_run_in_single_worker():
    pass
``` 

Now run with `pytest -n auto --dist=loadscope`.

Alternatively, for your specific user case specifically, where you have a single `test_redis.py` file which cannot run in concurrently, you can run the test suite in two batches:

1. Pass `-n auto -k -test_redis.py`: this excludes the entire `test_redis.py` from the run.
2. Pass `-n 0 -k test_redis.py`, this will run only the `test_redis.py` file, non-concurrently.

But I believe the solution using `loadscope` really should solve your problem.

My issue with that argument is that with the exception of datacenters (because we already have them in the SDK) you can have them as 3 different PR's because you enter them in to the BIG-IP as 3 different steps.

Where I think we went astray in this PR is we thought that multiple features in a PR was a good way to do business; when it is not. Consider any other open source project and the maintainers will no doubt reject PRs that encapsulate multiple features. That's what they were asking for here.

While the argument that "well if they all go in at the same time so that's the same as them each going in individually" is probably technically correct, it also makes the discussion about any of the code more scattered. We need to track discussion on 3 or more different topics instead of focusing on just one.

That's my view of the problem that the maintainers are trying to address here by asking for separate PRs. I wouldn't be able to get away with it in any other project that I contribute to, and one wouldn't get away with it when doing any BIG-IP development. So in the interests of maintaining focus and providing critical commentary about individual features, that's why it should be in separate commits.

Note that separate commits does not preclude the ability to merge everything at the same time. git is more than happy to branch off of a local branch you have that uses a previous feature you worked on as it's parent. During the commit process all of those can go in. But during the review process it's better if they are all separate.

Looking at the homepage, I'm not sure why that is the case. We're only fetching categories two levels deep. `get_absolute_urls` calls `get_ancestors_and_self`, which is then called a total about 10 times... Shouldn't produce near the query count we're seeing.

An easy fix is to construct the needed URL in the `category_tree` template tag (which is quite similar in approach to what @john-parton posted). But I'd like to understand why `get_ancestors_and_self` has such surprising performance characteristics.

Thank you for considering the proposal. How about if we could compromise with just enough of the runtime to pull the additional libraries from one location on the machine?

Here is how I think it could work. Let us say that the user written parts of the program consists of program.dll and library.dll.

program.exe = {bootstrap.dll + program.dll + library.dll}

Bootstrap.dll is a chunk of code that contains load on demand links for all the libraries referenced in program.dll and library.dll. It is also the code that is the entry point for the .exe.

When invoked, the Bootstrap.dll code searches for dotnet.ext the same as if it had been typed on the command line. Then it uses the dlls in the same directory to resolve all references for linked libraries.


The combined effect is that we now have single file executables that will run on any platform without modification[*]. The only condition being that the executable for dotnet (or some new loader assistant) has to be in the search path or discoverable by some defined search scheme.

[*] If the code requires platform specific code then it will be a different executable per platform of course. Cleaning up that issue can be left to a later point however.

@gelin Sorry for the delay in responding to this.

What exactly is the desired mitigation here? There's no rule for how to compare offset-naive datetimes with offset-aware datetimes. The options seem to be:

1. Convert `DTSTART` to `tzlocal()`
2. Convert `DTSTART` to `tzutc()`
3. Convert `DTSTART` to some other zone.
4. Convert `UNTIL` to naive.

You can see that 1, 2, and 3 are ambiguous with one another, and all three of those are ambiguous with 4. This seems to be a case of GIGO, and an error is appropriate.

@abalkin Is there actually enough information in the `tzfile` to solve this problem other than by using the dubious property of changes in the abbreviation? I know the `tzdata` inputs have a "SAVE" column, but that seems to be removed in the `zic`.

I suppose that the "next best solution" is to make two assumptions:

1. There is no real zone where the DST offset is == 0
2. There are no zones where both the DST offset and the base zone changed in  *both* the STD->DST transition *and* the DST->STD transition.

In which case, if we know it's DST from the `isdst` flag, and the calculated `dst()` is 0, then we should calculate the DST offset from the difference in offset with the _next_ standard time.

This still leaves us open to potential zones where the base zone change goes the other way (making the `dst()` value 2 hours instead of 1).

Hi @JeremyBYU,

I reviewed your PR, it's a good addition. Great that you included tests and some examples. I have some suggestions, let me know what you think. I hope you're still willing to work on this!

* Could you remove the .gitignore file from the branch? There currently is no .gitignore file in folium, if we add one it should be done separately.
* If you update from master the Travis tests should succeed.
* Currently part of the code is shared with `Marker` which would be good to avoid. Since this addition is only about icons, I think it would be best to model this class after the `Icon` class. That would be most similar to what users are currently used to:
```
BeautifyIcon(some_options).add_to(marker_object)
Marker(icon=BeautifyIcon(some_options))
```
* I would suggest to parse the options similarly as in `TileLayer`. Now the options are in two places: they are stored in self (e.g. `self.icon = icon`) and then in the template they are added to an `options` variable. Directly adding them to `self.options` in the `__init__` reduces lines. Furthermore the logic (if-statements) in the template are harder to read than if they were in the `__init__`. But I know this isn't done in folium consistently, and maybe there are also arguments against this, so I understand if you keep it like it is.
* When moving the options to `self.options`, the `has_number` and `is_alpha_numeric_icon` can maybe be removed, since they have only one line and are only used once. 
* Is `_get_self_bounds()` used anywhere?

This is could be problematic, or at least an issue to think about. Linux uses specific device names for serial ports (among many others), ttySxx is for 8250 (16550) like ports, ttymxcxx is for i.MX serial ports, ttyUSBXX is for USB serial ports that emulate the 8250. The reason for this is because differing hardware has differing capabilities, and the name of the port is intended to tell us which driver is in use, and therefore something about the port. While it is possible to trace the link backwards to the actual devnode and look at that to get some information, this is much more of a mess to pass back to the python programmer that is using the serial port. For example, it is likely not reasonable for PySerial to keep track of differing serial drivers, PySerial should be generic, and it really should be up to the programmer that is using PySerial to determine if the serial driver really has the expected capabilities. 
Here is an example:
In a perfect world, if I were to link /dev/ttymxc0 -> /dev/ttyS0, then use PySerial to open that port by passing in '/dev/ttyS0', then PySerial (should) tell us (by examining the devnode that the link points to) that this port is really not 8250 like and tell us that this port for example does not support mark or space parity like a 8250 port does. But we are likely not in a perfect world, and in this case it probably makes more sense to pass this responsibility to the programmer that is using PySerial. If symlinks are not supported, this forces the "port" to contain some information about the driver, and the programmer that is using PySerial can account for this...
Just my two cents worth. ;)

Right, my bad. I meant for row-major that iterating over column indices is fast as it's contiguous. But this means that the row lies contiguous in memory and can be quickly retrieved. I edited the statement above to not keep confusing people... 

I think your statements above make sense; I'd say you're right, single access to genes would be more frequent than to cells. Also, I believe you that it's better to have the dimension with more entries (cells, as nr. cells >> nr. genes) be the minor order; so genes as major order; I'll make some experiments with that... ;)

However, I'd say one should make the decision based on simple, clear conventions that should be the same between languages... I think both Python and R have the flexibility of interpreting arrays in memory as column- or row-major (as it's just a matter of the indexing convention) and hence, transpostitions can be avoided.

If loom files are ordered with genes as rows, Scanpy will be able to efficiently cope with that. Nonetheless, I think Scanpy should not stand against the conventions of the whole Python ecosystem and the stat/ML books; it will continue to present variables (genes) as columns to the user and observations/samples (cells) as rows, even though the notions "columns" and "rows" are never used - instead, after a long discussion, we decided for the names "var" for variables and "smp" for samples (maybe "obs" for observation in the future) to avoid running into these convention issues. Looking into the loom file, people might then get confused as things are called "rows" and "cols", but this might be something to live with.

The processing order from children to parents was a design decision, based on the assumption that ingredients should be independent of the experiment that uses them. That way each ingredient could be used by multiple experiments. And since it has been like that since the beginning, I don't really want to change it. 

But I do empathize with your usecase. I have often myself wanted to pass some information from the main experiment to an ingredient. This is part of the reason why I am not very happy with ingredients. I have some ideas for how to better solve this problem in the long term, but I currently don't have the time to do the major rewrite that this would require. 

So here is an idea for a workaround for your case: Make a verification function in algos that is not in any way captured by the ingredient (no hook, no config).  And then import and add that function as a config hook of your main experiment. Something like this:

``` python
# in algos.py
def algo_verify(config):
    if config['_source'] == 'source1':  # KeyError - '_source' is not yet initialized!
        assert config['_algo'] in set('algo1', 'algo3')
```

``` python
# in main.py
from algos import algos, algo_verify, algo1, algo2, algo3
ex.config_hook(algo_verify)
```

I've updated this PR with a few more commits as I'm slowly getting to grips with UI development and JavaScript unit testing.

1. moves functions in images.js to an object literal. As far as I can tell there is no getting around modularising the codebase if we want to develop and run unit tests. This is mainly because mocking libraries like [sinon](http://sinonjs.org) expect stubs and mocks to be created with ``mock(obj)`` or ``stub(object, "method")``.

2. as soon as we start to modularise, we're faced with having tests that only run in node.js, and production code that only works in browsers. As MMW serves as a browser-based UI, I think it makes sense to use [karma](https://karma-runner.github.io/0.13/index.html) to run all tests in a browser environment instead. Although we currently rely on PhantomJS for running tests, we could potentially look at leveraging [browserstack](https://www.browserstack.com) to troubleshoot and test corner cases on multiple platforms.

3. (2) above means that we need to use [browserify](http://browserify.org) to package everything up for karma and browser environments.

4. (3) implies that we ultimately need a build step that does ``./node_modules/.bin/browserify mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/*.js -o mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/bundle.js``, and then just include ``bundle.js`` in the html files and mb.appcache. The upside is that we can then minimise everything before shipping. The downside is requiring developers to have node installed on their development machines, and adding build script to ``package.json``, but at least we can then get rid of ``nodeenv`` which should make tox runs much faster.

5. as modularising the code will be a gradual process, we'll need workarounds like including the following at the end of our script files until we can run everything through browserify:
```Javascript
if (typeof exports !== 'undefined') {
    if (typeof module !== 'undefined' && module.exports) {
        module.exports = coverArt
    }
}
```
...and also loading all of the production code (``'mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/**/*.js'``) into the karma run until we can ``require('./just_the_libraries_we_need.js')`` for test cases.

But that's it. If everyone can live with the above then I think we have a way of moving forward, increasing test coverage, and modularising the MMW codebase to make it easier to maintain in future.

P.S. The first test coverage report is available at [https://coveralls.io/builds/5432742](https://coveralls.io/builds/5432742).

There are expressions, sorts and function declarations. 
Expressions have sorts, sorts are sorts themselves, and function declarations use domains and ranges, so what is the sort of that? Is it the range? then there is a function to access that.

The C API overloads the use of AST: it is used consistently for expressions. For sorts, the API uses the Z3_sort and for function declarations it uses Z3_func_decl. So there are many contexts where API functions take Z3_ast as input and expect these to be expressions. The Z3_get_sort is one of these functions. > As far as I can see, this means I have to fit the network one image at a time, since storing images as a batch means they would all have to be the same size. Is this assumption correct?

Basically yes. It's the same problem you get when processing a batch of differently-sized temporal sequences with an RNN. A common solution in that case is to zero-pad the sequences to have the same size and ensure the zero-padded data does not participate in computing the loss.

> Can you think of a way to speed this up - or is this issue better approached in Theano?

One way would be propagating all images in parallel until they passed the pooling layer, then concatenating the data into a batch. However, this requires Theano to use different streams, which is only supported by the new backend. The code would probably look something like this:
```python
inputs = ...  # a list of input variables for your batch
network1 = ...  # the part up to and including the spatial pyramid pooling
network2 = ...  # the remaining part, connected to network1
intermediate = T.concatenate([lasagne.layers.get_output(network1, T.shape_padleft(inp, 1), deterministic=False) for inp in inputs], axis=0)  # not sure whether you'd need to do anything to ensure it uses different streams
final = lasagne.layers.get_output(network2, {network1: intermediate}, deterministic=False)
```

Alternatively, you can borrow the zero-padding technique from RNNs. You'd have to zero-pad everything to the same size, then crop the images before the pyramid pooling layer and pass them through pooling one by one (you may also be able to write a custom pyramid pooling layer that does this via `scan`). This would at least give you full parallelization of the convolutions, at the cost of some wasted computation on the padded data.

Oh. You're right, it doesn't make sense the way it's documented.
For reference, the hinge loss is `max(0, delta - predictions * targets)`, with delta defaulting to 1.

If the predictions are sigmoid in (0,1), then for a negative target, the loss will always be between delta and delta + 1. That's not intended.
If the predictions are tanh in (-1,1), then for the default delta of 1, the `max(0, ...)` does not make sense. That's also not necessarily intended.
To be equivalent to an SVM, you'd actually feed the log-odds (before applying the sigmoid): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinge_loss

I think rather than changing the implementation, we should change the documentation to encourage feeding the log-odds (sometimes called logits).

The world frame is defined differently in the raw vs. odometry ground truth poses. In the odometry poses, the world frame is defined such that it coincides with the pose of the camera in the first image of the sequence. This is why the first ground truth pose is always the identity transformation in the odometry sequences. In the raw sequences, the ground truth poses are the poses of the OXTS sensor, not the camera, and pykitti retains the East-North-Up coordinate system for the orientation.

So to get from raw ground truth poses to odometry ground truth poses, you need to first convert the IMU poses to camera poses (which you already did), but then also transform all the camera poses so that they are relative to the first camera pose. 

Here's some pseudocode that may be helpful. I tacked on `_odo` or `_raw` to indicate where each pose came from, and `[0]` refers to the first image/pose.

```python
# Convert raw IMU poses to raw cam0 poses
T_w_cam0_raw = T_w_imu_raw * inv(T_cam0_imu) 

# Convert raw cam0 poses to odometry (relative) cam0 poses
T_w_cam0_odo =  inv(T_w_cam0_raw[0]) * T_w_cam0_raw pose
```

I tried this and was able to recover odometry poses from raw poses, give or take some rounding error.

We have another approach in DRF integration. For example we have the following model & serializer:

```python

class Expenses(models.Model):
    amount = MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)


class Serializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Expenses
        fields = '__all__'
```

And serialization works in the following way:

```python
>>> instance = Expenses.objects.create(amount=Money(10, 'EUR'))
>>> serializer = Serializer(instance=instance)
>>> serializer.data
ReturnDict([
    ('id', 1),
    ('amount_currency', 'EUR'),
    ('amount', '10.000'),
])
```
In other words built-in field serializes only amount part of money instance, currency is serialized automatically by setting ``fields = '__all__'``. It is different from your implementation, which will create a nested structure.

And deserialization works in the same way - it works with money instance, not with nested structure.

If you need nested approach, then we could add some configuration setting for it.

@amttr Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying the misuses do not exist. In fact, they have always been a big problem in CLE. I'm sure we got a lot of things wrong due to our ignorance. However, the current logic in `_get_safe_rebase_addr()` is buggy in the sense that it will create "holes" when multiple libraries are loaded and are always rebased using the most recent maximum address in the process. I experienced that issue, and I don't think that will be mitigated by your PR. Let me know if you disagree with this.

I'm not saying you should address it. If it is a real issue (which I believe it is), it can be a separate issue anyway.

> I hope Angr can construct CFG correctly without the explicit check code. In many cases, we cannot change the source of the tested program, so we cannot add the explict check manually.

Recovering the CFG from machine code is not as easy as it seems to be. In your example (the buggy one), `(*fun[0])()`, `(*fun[-1])()`, and `(*fun[13337])()` are all equivalent to the CPU, and apparently, they are all possible targets for that call.

angr will be able to resolve the jump table in `callsites()` *with heuristics* once the jump table resolution is fully implemented in `CFGAccurate`. Heuristics are essential here in order to identify the base address of the function pointer array, which might work for a lot of real-world cases, but is impossible to work for all such cases.

For this specific example, I believe the best way is to rely on the jump table resolution of `CFGAccurate`. I'll work on it later this week.

> If the tested program is not written by me, and I get a warning like  `WARNING | 2016-05-09 13:44:43,902 | simuvex.s_run | Exit state has over 257 possible solutions. Likely unconstrained; skipping. <BV64 reg_18_47_64> .` How can I locate and diagnose the bug?

`CFGAccurate` is not designed to be a vulnerability finder. You can modify angr's code (start from `CFGAccurate._get_simrun()` in `cfg_accurate.py`) in order to catch this specific case.

However, having too many possible solutions does not necessarily mean the existence of a bug. It could also mean some indirect jump is not easily recoverable. You'll have to make some policies in order to use `CFGAccurate` to detect bugs/vulnerabilities.

> It does work no matter adding context_sensitivity_level or call_depth.

I assume you mean "it does not work even when context_sensitivity_level or call_depth is used". (If I remember correctly), as an optimization (for speed), symbolic back traversal does not go back beyond function boundaries. It also has a limit of how many basic blocks angr should traverse back. In your case, I think it's likely that angr does not traverse back enough number of basic blocks to see your constraint on `i`. You can adjust some parameters inside `CFGAccurate._symbolically_back_traverse()` to make it work in this case.

The line of code you mentioned is not the problem. [`out_queue`is a Queue object](https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/mavlink.py#L132), which is defined in the [`queue` module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/queue.html#queue.Queue.get).

From the documentation:

> The queue module implements multi-producer, multi-consumer queues. It is especially useful in threaded programming when information must be exchanged safely between multiple threads. The Queue class in this module implements all the required locking semantics. It depends on the availability of thread support in Python; see the threading module.

> `Queue.get(block=True, timeout=None)`
> Remove and return an item from the queue. If optional args block is true and timeout is None (the default), block if necessary until an item is available. If timeout is a positive number, it blocks at most timeout seconds and raises the Empty exception if no item was available within that time. Otherwise (block is false), return an item if one is immediately available, else raise the Empty exception (timeout is ignored in that case).

So, this has nothing to do with network latency.

@waTeim Essentially there is no way to do this right in every case. The autopilot acknowledges only some commands (those sent with a commandlong) and that acknowledgement is pretty useless in that 

1. it only tells you that something was invalid or was received - it doesn't tell you that the command succeeded or failed.
2. The command format is lossy, so you can't guarantee the message will arrive at the vehicle or that you will receive the acknowledgement.
3. There is no way to associate a message to its acknowledgement - though it is most likely that as long as you aren't sending messages too quickly (and that the script and the autopilot are the only things communicating on the network) that the current ack will match last sent. 

And yes, it is also quite possible that messages will be swamped. In general commands are replaced by later commands "silently".

*Tentatively*, yes, I'm opposed to having an optional native extension.

My thinking on this is a bit complex, so let me lay it out.

1. Generally speaking I have a policy regarding C code, which is that wherever possible I'd like to avoid having untrusted input reach C code. The risks of running C code on untrusted input are too high, and I find that generally speaking I and most software developers I work with are bad at spotting places where assumptions are made that data will be "reasonable". That makes C a great vector for bugs, and in particular for the kind of bug that does not affect a pure-Python program.

    For that reason, if we were going to write a native extension I'd want it to be in Rust, not C. However,

2. The wheel ecosystem is not quite there yet. While it's now mostly possible to ship binary wheels for most platforms, there are a number of Linux distributions that cannot use manylinux1 wheels. This is a problem if you want to ship a Rust extension, because it's very difficult to indicate the need for a Rust compile chain on a random Linux system.

    This problem also affects C extensions, by the way: one of the systems that doesn't support a manylinux1 wheel is Alpine, which uses musl libc. That means that if we shipped a C extension on that platform, we'd require the installation of a C toolchain for users to install the module: not great.

3. So the only option is, as you said, to make the module optional: we'd ideally want to check whether we could compile it and, if we couldn't, we'd simply fall back to pure-Python code.

    I have *extremely* mixed feelings about this, most of them not good. In particular, it's a bit of a hairy debugging issue: what code the user is executing becomes conditional on difficult-to-introspect features of their platform; we double the number of code paths that need to be tested and debugged; and we double the workload for feature addition and API changes.

The TL;DR here, I think, is that right now I'm about -0.25 on having a native extension. If we could come up with a nice Rust extension then I'd reconsider, but I'd really rather not maintain a C extension if it can possibly be avoided.

First, let's note that there are two locations where the ``schema`` attribute was introduced:
* on the collection object, for the JSON schema definition
* on records, for the validated schema version

How it was done
--------------------

Regarding the collection object, I found it consistent to put it in ``data``. As @Natim said:

> In this implementation, we are just providing a new property to the collection which is the schema.
> Other property may come such as signing, description, title.

The schema *is* the collection data, it is completely consistent :) Because data is just the name we gave to the main *payload* of response, and has no further semantics. That's what kinto.js reads when it receives the response. 

Plus it was very easy to implement, since completely in the rails of current cliquet resource code.

As for the record, since we wanted to have a way to filter records by schema version, it had to become a record attribute. In cliquet, we store the data content in the storage backend (*which allows filtering*) and the permissions in the permissions backend.

Removing the odd
-----------------------

Now. I fully understand your concern : keeping the application specific properties in ``data`` and every Kinto notion at the root level is a good *(new)* idea !

But IMO, in order to remain consistent, we should then also move ``id`` and ``last_modified`` ! 

Collection would look like this:

```js
{
   "id": "087-7u8097908708",
   "last_modified": 456789986,
   "fingerprint": "11:D5:D2:0A:9A:F8:D9:FC:23:6E:5C",
   "data": {
       "author": "Bozo the clown",
   },
  "permissions": {
     "write": ["fxa:00567y09"]
  },
  "schema": {
    ....json-schema...
  }
}
```

Same idea for records:

```js
{
   "id": "087-7u8097908708",
   "last_modified": 78945144,
   "fingerprint": "11:D5:D2:0A:9A:F8:D9:FC:23:6E:5C",
   "data": {
       "title": "F Society",
       "url": "http://thefreedictionary.com/decentralize",
   },
  "permissions": {
     "write": ["fxa:00567y09"]
  },
  "schema": 456789986
}
```

But, hey, this is not a minor task. It does not look like one of our priority either. And it would break kinto.js AFAIK. 

Even though I find it attractive, I am not sure I want to put a foot in this energy absorbing swirl :) This is pretty huge.

Pragmatism
----------------

In order to implement your idea without inverting too much effort, we could : 

* leave ``id`` and ``last_modified`` as they are
* leave the ``schema`` attribute on records as it is
* move the ``schema`` attribute to the root of collection object

But, does it really bring any value ? Will it be fondamentally better ? What are our criterias to evaluate that ?

Please note that it would be a lot cleaner if we refactor some part of cliquet resource schema manipulation in order to achieve this. Otherwise payload validation (especially for PATCH request) [will have even more ugly parts](https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/master/cliquet/resource.py#L176-L178). *I would be motivated to do it though*.

> relying on Total-Records to do correct pagination is essentially always wrong

Yes! That!

Pagination is best done with a cursor-technique. Like, if there is a Next-URL header, keep going. Any client that tries to do...:
```python
res = requests.get(url)
all=[res.json()]
pages = int(res.headers['Total-Records'] / 10000) + 1
for p in range(pages):
    res = requests.get(res.headers['next-url'])
    all.extend(res.json())
```
...is doomed to fail and burn. 

Category 3 talks about "could stick a constant value in there" which I think is best avoided. I'd vote for something like:
```python
if request.headers.get('prefer')=='include-total-records' or request.method == 'HEAD' or request.query.get('include')=='total-records':
    response.set_header('Total-Records', self._count_records(collection_id, parent_id, filters))
else:
    response.set_header('Total-Records-Excluded', "See documentation if you want the 'Total-Records' header")
```

Yeah, we agree it's a suboptimal solution. However, the rawgit.com says this on their website:

> Excessive traffic will be throttled and blacklisted. If excessive traffic continues, RawGit will display a prominent error message on your website to try to get your attention.

And the issue is that we can't control how often our users hit the `.../master/patches.json` URL. It's possible that someone doesn't pay attention and leaves a script running for hours that hits the URL over and over, and rawgit.com then blacklists the URL for **all** users. That would be a much worse problem! Then none of the users for Cass or Orianna could use the patches.json file, and in fact the code would throw an error that the user has little control over. In fact, someone could also maliciously ping that URL, and we wouldn't even know it nor could do anything about it.

We've decided that it's unacceptable to provide that kind of instability to our users, and have instead opted for the current solution.

That said, we are _actively_ working on fixing this issue permanently. Last week we put in quite a bit of work towards that goal, so hopefully sometime in the semi-near future we'll have this issue fixed once and for all.

@sbrodehl Thanks a million for helping us with these issues. We really appreciate it.

It's relatively normal to get 500s. I get them quite a bit, and retrying a couple times usually does the trick. It's just a temporary downtime in the Riot API that is unavoidable.

However, you definitely should not be exceeding the rate limits with those numbers. Did you double check your developer key limits? I can't believe they wouldn't be 10/10 and 500/600, but maybe yours are off for some reason (or maybe they reduced them due to too many 429s before?). 

>But if I run the code for more than about 30~50games it crashes and gives me 500 error.

Check out our `auto_retry` function (and how it's used (you can copy-paste)) in one of our examples:  https://github.com/meraki-analytics/cassiopeia/blob/master/example/match_collection.py  I use this all the time for projects like yours when I need to pull a bunch of data and I want to retry 500s a few times. Hopefully that will solve most of your issues. After a few hours I often get two 500s in a row that the program still quits, but that's just something you might have to deal with unless you want to really stress out Riot's servers (which we don't recommend).

I have no idea what's going on with your rate limit though, so be careful with that. We don't want you to get blacklisted. A few users have had similar problems, but they have decided it was their fault rather than Cass's. Hopefully it's just a number change, and it's clear when you turn on `print_calls` (see below). We'd be happy to help you figure it out though if you keep providing us with helpful. information.

>Though I read that cass offers "automatic databasing", I was getting a feeling that I'm still missing sth silly to take advantage of this "databasing" concept.

In order to use the databasing that Cass offers, you have to have some sort of database set up first, and then connect Cass to it. We desperately need some documentation on that, and we are going to try to write some in the next week or two. There a few options for types of databases that you can choose, and I don't know enough about them personally to recommend any. See here: http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/engines.html   One thing you can also do is create an in-memory-only database, which will be destroyed when your program quits. If you want a persistent database, you will have to set one up on your computer first, then connect SQLAlchemy to it within your python program (see the constructor at https://github.com/meraki-analytics/cassiopeia/blob/master/cassiopeia/type/api/store.py#L203). Doing this will allow you to keep a record of all the data you have pulled. I don't know if I would recommend doing this right off the bat unless you are trying to pull thousands and thousands of games and use them multiple times. If you choose to go with the in-memory database (Ctrl+F "memory" here: http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/engines.html), you will need to be careful that you do not pull so much data that your computers memory fills up, or your computer will crash. Essentially, if your memory will fill up then you will have to use a persistent database (i.e. one that saves the data permanently on your computer), but if you only need the data once and you don't need to keep it all in memory the entire time, then an in-memory cache should work.

Despite all that discussion about databasing, Cass still does some automatic caching on it's own. If you turn `riotapi.print_calls(True)` on, you will be able to see every call that Cass makes to Riot. If you run

    riotapi.get_champions()
    riotapi.get_champion_by_name("Annie")

you will see that on the first call, Cass gets the data from Riot. However, on the second call, Cass recognizes that it already has Annie, and pulls it from the cache. This is independent of any database you install. Also, you can use this `print_calls` function to help figure out what's going on with all your requests. (You should also see it pause every ~10 calls to wait for the rate limit quite often.)


>I'm just really new to this whole api and db thing. I just know that there is a call limit in riot api and that using db can resolve the issue. That is why I really appreciate your library.

Thanks! We are glad you like it and find it useful. It is written for exactly people like you (and us) who want to use the API but not deal with all the un-fun stuff. We just want to access the data and not have to worry about the intricacies of the API itself. That's what Cass is all about.

>But then... I do not really know how to take advantage of the db...

This is in part our fault due to lack of documentation. We know this really needs to be done, but we have been backlogged on a few other important projects recently. However, we plan to get much better documentation in the next week or two! In the mean time, hopefully what was posted above helps. If not, we'll keep trying to help out.

`graphql-core` also supports py3.5 asyncio, graphene's resolve functions should also support `async/await` in py35. 

```python
class Human(Character):
    pet = Field(Pet)

    async def resolve_pet(self, *args):
        pet = await queryPetFromSomewhereWithOwner(self.instance.id)
        return Pet(pet)
```
I think you can get this info from the query AST, but there should be a more convenient way to get this.

All I need is to pass the property id and room id to my service to get the room details. Going through the property is an unnecessary job because it will pass in the ```get_node``` method from the ```Property``` node. Since I'm using ```DataLoader``` the data is in cache but even then I don't want my code to go through unnecessary methods to fetch the data.
I would prefer to use a room graphene field at root and translate the global id to proper id using the ```from_global_id``` method

Still super slow. So let me summarize what we know:

@anselor has a linux distro using system python, which imports a bunch of extra modules when you import `cmd2`, resulting in long wall time to do the import. @kotfu noticed a similar phenomenon on macOS using `pyenv` and `virtualenv`. @kotfu fixed his problem by re-creating the virtualenv. @anselor got rid of PYTHON_PATH in favor of an editable install, and reinstalled all the packages, but the issue persists. @anselor tried it on a different VM and did not observe the issue.

@kotfu made some changes in the *speedup_import* branch, which just defer importing certain infrequently used modules until the user takes some action which requires the module. This can reduce the cycles required to import `cmd2`, but these changes do not address the root cause, and only make incremental improvement.

Based on these facts, it appears that we still don't understand the root cause of the issue. We have a couple of instances where it occurs, but it doesn't always occur. My current best guess is that this is caused by something outside of `cmd2` code.

I think this is the only issue holding up release of 0.9.0, I guess we need to decide if we want to continue to hold up the release until we get this resolved. Options for how to move forward are:

1. move forward with release of 0.9.0 without merging *speedup_import* and indicate in the changelog that we have this potential issue under investigation
2. merge *speedup_import* and release 0.9.0, with a similar note in changelog that we are investigating this issue
3. continue to investigate this issue and get it resolved before releasing 0.9.0

I'm leaning towards option 1. What does everybody else think?

@mgiaco 
Hi,

In its default behavior a ``cmd2`` application using the built-in ``cmdloop`` method is a **line-oriented command interpreter**.  What this means is that the main thread will block waiting for the user to enter a command and press **<Enter>** (technically it is actually running code within the C readline library that supports tab-completion and such and not purely blocking).  Once the user presses **<Enter>**, then the command line is interpreted and the desired command is executed.  Once that command finishes executing, a new prompt is issued and the loop blocks again waiting for the user to enter a command and press **<Enter>**.

So even if you have a separate thread populating the ``cmdqueue``, no commands placed into that queue will execute until the main loop unblocks.

If you desire different behavior, then you should probably not use the built-in ``cmdloop`` and implement your own main loop which would in turn rely on either ``runcmds_plus_hooks`` or ``onecmd_plus_hooks`` for the actual command execution.

Hmm. I think Anymail *does* need to re-cast that requests exception as an AnymailRequestsAPIError (in anymail.backends.BaseRequests.post_to_esp). That's required for proper fail_silently handling.

There are several Django packages that maintain an email queue and robustly retry failed sends at a later time. Anymail should be compatible with most of them (any that let you change their email backend). 

* I personally use the old [Pinax django-mailer](https://github.com/pinax/django-mailer), which runs in Django's DB and works fine at my relatively-small scale. (Also, it seems to be getting maintained again -- yay!). 

* [Celery](http://www.celeryproject.org/) is popular for managing any sort of task queues in Django, and there are a bunch of Django email queues based on it.

* I'm sure there are others. (Including some interesting [explorations using Django channels](https://www.wordfugue.com/using-django-channels-email-sending-queue/).)

Queue-and-resend functionality isn't built into Anymail, because there are different approaches that satisfy different needs, and the other packages cover the tradeoffs well. (Also, Anymail doesn't itself maintain any state or run any async tasks.)

The `undef` is a workaround to enable building a `debug` extension against a `release` Python. This is possible on other platforms but usually not on Windows because of that `pragma` in `pyconfig.h`. Building just the extension in debug mode is very useful because most Python distributions don't include the debug libraries by default.

If all you want is to build a debug version of your extension and you don't want to dive into Python internals, it's fine to build against the release version of Python. If you really want to debug Python internals, the special debug builds will usually report the correct debug `.lib` in CMake, or you can manually override the `PYTHON_LIBRARIES` variable.

> However, even though it has pybind11 as a dependency, it doesn't seem to automatically locate the pybind11 header files. What's the canonical way to find them, so I can add them to the include_dirs member of the Extension?

@xoltar [pbtest](https://github.com/pybind/pbtest) is a good start to create a pip-installable module that depends on pybind11.

If the pybind11 package is installed correctly, its header files should have been copied in a subdirectory called `pybind11` of your python include directory, where Python.h is for example.

*Note that `pip install pybind11` will indeed place these headers correctly but not `python setup.py install` because of a limitation of setuptools. (pip runs the setup.py script but with a certain flag called `--single-version-externally-managed`)*

To locate the headers in your extension module, you need to specify the directory as the main headers directory of python, that is `'include'`

```python
ext_modules = [
    Extension(
        'pbtest',
        [the list of your cpp files],
        include_dirs=['include'],
        language='c++',
    ),
]
```

Also, more information about why we are using Mozilla's recommendations can be found [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/ciphers.html), however, this page is currently unlisted from our documentation and some of the information there like planning to make the cipher recommendation used configurable is out of date.

In Certbot's next minor release, we are planning on adding hook directories to Certbot to allow users and package maintainers to add scripts to be run during certificate renewal by simply placing an executable file in the appropriate directory. We wanted to post the proposed design of this system publicly to get feedback on it and to make sure it covers the majority of the desired use cases. If you are not already familiar with hooks in Certbot, see the documentation about the --pre-hook, --deploy-hook, and --post-hook flags found at https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewing-certificates.

The proposed design is to create a directory named something like "renew-hooks" in Certbot's configuration directory (which is located at /etc/letsencrypt by default). Inside of this directory are three additional directories called "pre", "deploy", and "post" where hooks of each type can be placed. Certbot will create this directories itself if not already done so by the packaging system that installed Certbot.

When Certbot is run with the "renew" subcommand and there are certificates due for renewal, all executable files found in these directories will be run like other hooks of the same type and will run in addition to any hooks defined through existing means such as the command line, INI configuration file, or renewal configuration file. Hooks defined in these directories will not be stored in renewal configuration files, but they will continue to be run when certificates are renewed with the "renew" subcommand as long as they remain in the appropriate directory.

Hooks defined in these directories will only be run with the "renew" subcommand and will be ignored when running with the "certonly" or "run" subcommands. If you want to use hooks with these other subcommands, you will have to define them on the command line or Certbot INI file.

If anyone has any feedback on this plan, we'd love to hear it!

This is harder to properly fix as I thought, as it seems renders are handling these border cases differently and we can't know what behavior the user expects.

Let's start with something simple
```SVG
<text>
text1
text2
text3
</text>
```
According to https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/text.html#WhiteSpace the renderer is supposed to remove all newlines - i.e. the rendered result should be "text1text2text3". Inkscape does this, Firefox renders "text1 text2 text3" instead. This is already problematic as we do not know which behavior the user expected so we can't now wether it's fine to optimize this into `<text>text1text2text3</text>` or not (it is according to spec).

Now add some indentation
```SVG
<text>
    text1
    text2
    text3
</text>
```
According to spec contiguous space characters shall be consolidated, i.e. expected rendering result is "text1 text2 text3". At least now it's clear wether there are spaces intended or not... Here's some (safe) room for optimization in Scour as we currently do not touch contiguous spaces.

Now with the added `<tspan>` it get's more complicated (I'm still trying to figure out that part):
```SVG
<text>
text1
<tspan>text2</tspan>
text3
</text>
```
Should this still render as "text1text2text3"? Inkscape does, Firefox adds spaces...

Right now I'm considering two solutions: 
* Keep the current logic but remove indentation. This will still result in spaces when rendered in Firefox though. Shall we blame this on Firefox? Or do we need a workaround?
* The workaround could be to determine wether the original document had line breaks and optimize accordingly, i.e. stick to the original document. This is ugly, as it basically comes down to assuming `xml:space="preserve"` for text nodes by default (which is wrong). Unfortunately it might be the solution with the least unexpected behavior for users.

I have essentially completed it at https://github.com/levic/factory_boy/commits/wip/auto_factory
I wasn't planning to do a pull request until I've used it for a week in a production project in case the test cases missed something (unless you want to replace the `wip/auto_factory` branch in the main repo)

**General Changes**
- It will now look at `null`/`blank`/`default` and only create default field definitions when it looks like `full_clean()` or `save()` will fail
- If `field.choices` is set then it will randomly pick from the available choices regardless of the field type
- Removed conditional support for django < 1.8 (1.7 ended maintenance 2 years ago)
- Removed code you wrote for `OptionDefault` parent dict merging, and low/high fuzzy number generation that wasn't used. I extracted them out as the last 2 commits in [this branch]
 (https://github.com/levic/factory_boy/commits/wip/auto_factory_extra), they're not in the main auto_factory branch

**Factory Interface changes**
- `auto_fields` has been split into `default_auto_fields`/`include_auto_fields`/`exclude_auto_fields`
```
class MyFactory:
        class Meta:
            model = MyModel
            default_auto_fields = True
            include_auto_fields = ['extra_field1', 'extra_field2']
            exclude_auto_fields = ['ignore_this_field']

```
- `default_auto_fields` - if True then the default set of fields will be included
- `include_auto_fields` - tells the introspector to additionally autogenerate definitions for these fields. If `default_auto_fields` is False then fields listed in `include_auto_fields` will still be included
- `exclude_auto_fields` - tells the introspector to never autogenerate definitions for these fields
- The rationale behind this is to allow for easier inheriting of settings for abstract factories (eg to have a base class in your application that has a blacklist of fields that are always included/excluded regardless of the model).

**Introspector Interface Changes**
- In my particular use case I want to first of all look at field names and only if these don't match then fall back to looking at field types. This was possible before but was a bit clumsy. I've changed the interface so that `build_declaration` is easier to override and do this (more of the logic that you shouldn't need to override is now in `build_declarations` -- [here](https://github.com/levic/factory_boy/commit/a93ee3e) )

**Including all Fields**
- I looked at making `'*'` an option to `include_auto_fields`, but there are still fields you don't want to include (eg reverse foreign keys, AutoField).
    - Instead I made a variant introspector; see the code [here](https://github.com/levic/factory_boy/commit/9d20fa15018ca727f9290b6b588a1ac9378354e9#diff-7c928ff073a9075cc628be362f42a7f7R1014) for an example. It is not as succinct as just including `'*'` but it substantially simplifies the internal implementation.

**Outstanding Issues**
- I have looked at the logic in model mommy and added test cases for issues that they came across, but haven't tested [generic relations](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/contrib/contenttypes/#generic-relations) (there is code in there but it may or may not actually work)
- No documentation update. Will try to do this sometime in the next week or two before the pull request
