```python
class AlazarParameter(Parameter):
    """
    This class represents of many parameters that are relevant for the Alazar
    driver. This parameters only have a private set method, because the values
    are set by the Alazar driver. They do have a get function which return a
    human readable value. Internally the value is stored as an Alazar readable
    value.

    These parameters also keep track the up-to-dateness of the value of this
    parameter. If the private set_function is called incorrectly, this parameter
    raises an error when the get_function is called to warn the user that the
    value is out-of-date

    Args:
        name: see Parameter class
        label: see Parameter class
        unit: see Parameter class
        instrument: see Parameter class
        value: default value
        byte_to_value_dict: dictionary that maps byte values (readable to the
            alazar) to values that are readable to humans
        vals: see Parameter class, should not be set if byte_to_value_dict is
            provided
    """
    def __init__(self, name=None, label=None, unit=None, instrument=None,
                 value=None, byte_to_value_dict=None, vals=None):

        super().__init__(name=name, label=label, units=unit, vals=vals)

I've automated this as part of my CI/CD process. Hope someone else finds this useful until the underlying issue is resolved.

```bash
# Workaround for Pulumi Issue with modifications to Cloudfront - see: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1449
pulumi stack export > before.json 
qualifiedString=$(cat before.json | jq '.deployment.resources[] | select(.type | contains("aws:cloudfront/distribution:Distribution")) | "URN: \( .urn ) ID: \( .id )"')
distId=$(echo $qualifiedString| cut -d ' ' -f 4 | tr -d \")
echo "Looking up tag for " $distId
etagValue=`perl -nle 'print $& if m{ETag": "\K([A-Z0-9]+)}' <<< $(aws cloudfront get-distribution --id $distId | grep -i etag)`
echo "Found tag: " $etagValue
(cat before.json | jq --arg DISTID "$distId" --arg ETAG "$etagValue" '.deployment.resources = (.deployment.resources | (.[] | select(.id == $DISTID) | .outputs.etag) |= $ETAG)') > updated.json
pulumi stack import < updated.json
rm updated.json
rm before.json
```

```py
# server.py
from threading import Thread
import socket
import streamlink
import sys


def server_thread(socks):
    serv = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    serv.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    serv.bind(('localhost', 5000))
    serv.listen()
    while True:
        sock, addr = serv.accept()
        print('New sock from ', addr)
        socks.append(sock)


def reader_thread(url, socks):
    streams = streamlink.streams(url)
    io = streams['best'].open()
    while True:
        buf = io.read(4096)
        if not buf:
            break
        for sock in socks:
            try:
                sock.send(buf)
            except:
                # TODO remove
                pass

socks = []
t1 = Thread(target=server_thread, args=(socks,))
t1.start()
t2 = Thread(target=reader_thread, args=(sys.argv[1], socks))
t2.start()

t1.join()
t2.join()
```

The fact that this even displays properly in discord the way you are demonstrating isn't even a guarantee. The code block specification as used by discord is literally
`````
```[syntax specifier]
zero or more lines to be highlighted by the syntax specified.
```
`````
So, this is just a misuse of the code blocks.

Here's a dumb example of why we this isn't even worth attempting to detect.
`````
[p]eval ```py = "Hello World!"
print(py)```
`````

py is a valid highlight syntax. It's also being used as a variable in the code. Without putting abstract syntax parsing into the eval code, there's not a good way to determine which applies, and that is a lot of extra overhead to try and support users misusing code blocks.

You have a valid point, it could be clearer how to use custom reasons.

To specify a custom reason, use `[p]warn <user> custom` and the bot will ask you to enter the reason, along with some other stuff I believe.

To make it more user-friendly, perhaps when custom reasons are enabled, changing the message "That is not a registered reason!" to "That is not a registered reason! To specify a custom reason, put `custom` for the reason."?

> Is there any other way I can send data from javascript to python and wait for it to arrive?

There isn't infrastructure to deal with having python code pause execution and wait for events from javascript (except for the raw_input/input python builtin functions to get raw strings from the front end). Options for dealing with this include: (a) using a callback-based system, i.e., register a callback to continue your code and end your current execution, or (b) start another thread with your computation and return from the main thread and use some sort of locking primitive and a callback handler on the main thread to unblock the other thread, or (c) do some async/await magic to make the callback-based system in (a) look like synchronous code (I think this would work...).

2. Specify that model specs are the exact version number of the actual data (e.g., `1.0.0`), *not* a semver range, like `^1.0`. > "Deprecate for a month" doesn't work if there was no release within that month

Right, if we have a "deprecate for X time" policy, then the timer should start counting from the first release that includes the deprecation, not from the first commit that includes the deprecation.

> finding and removing deprecated code isn't any more or less work whether you do it sooner or later.

There is some overhead to having deprecated code hanging around the codebase though, and the bookkeeping to keep track of which stuff can be removed when is overhead too. (The more times we go "hey can we remove this? Oh not yet", the more attention we're wasting.) It's not necessarily a big deal, but like here we've just had a contributor's PR closed because of it; Laura is doing extra work because of this policy right now!

> server

For this project in particular, our challenge is to become a stable foundation library as quickly as we can. I would love it if we could release a 1.0 within, say, a year. But to do this we need more users to gain confidence in our API, which means that between now and 1.0 we'll need to do a juggling act where as more users gradually come on board, we gradually get stricter about stability so that the next round of users feels comfortable joining us... So it's a little more complex than "semver says that anything goes".

And all the issues he brings up there have other solutions – our CI already builds, installs, and runs the tests against the installed version on every PR; we already get combined coverage on CI runs through codecov, it's really *only* the CI runs that can give useful coverage because we need to combine across multiple operating systems. Hm, okay, let me try to put my thoughts differently:

- Having aclose() in the generic async stream interface only makes sense if a graceful close makes sense for every kind of async stream. So assume this is the case.
- In your own words, the aclose() implementation for SocketStream currently only behaves correctly for clients, but not for servers. So aclose() is actually going to do the wrong thing in one out of two cases.
- However, at least in plain HTTP clients don't even need to do a graceful close - they can just close the connection. So arguably, the aclose() method fails in all the cases where it makes sense to call it (when you're acting as a server).
- As far as I can see, there isn't any reason for the client to behave differently when using SSL. Why bother with a long shutdown if you can just close the socket after you received the data you wanted? Yet for SSL case the `aclose` method actually does behave graceful. 

So when deciding whether to call aclose(), we actually have the following decision tree:

Am I the client or server?
1. When client, is it plain HTTP or SSL?
  1.a. if plain HTTP, use aclose if you want speed, or a custom method if you want to be nice to the server
  1.b if SSL, use a custom method if you want speed, or aclose if you want to be nice to the server, 
2. When server, is it plain HTTP or SSL?
  2.a. if plain HTTP, use a custom method
  2.b if SSL, use aclose

I think this makes aclose() rather awkward to use in its current form. I think one way to make it easier to use would be to have aclose() *always* behave gracefully. That way you can just always use it and nothing bad will happen. And if you happen to be client and need some extra speed, you have the *option* to call close() instead.

simplejson isn't designed to be used for javascript code generation, it's designed to consume and produce (only) valid JSON.

For cycle breaking you should transform your data before trying to serialize it.

To generate invalid JSON you should fork simplejson or find another library that's more malleable for your needs.
